figures.caption: Table 2: Performance metrics for phenotyping algorithms in the non-rare, informative scenario with simplified data. This represents the most favorable conditions with higher disease prevalence and sufficient informative features.

Figure 1: Automated EHR phenotyping workflow with parallel processing. Silver-standard labels from electronic health records feed into PheNorm and MAP methods in parallel. PheNorm uses weakly-supervised denoising with dropout regression and EM estimation. MAP employs multimodal ensemble learning with mixture models. sureLDA integrates both approaches: using PheNorm probabilities as Dirichlet priors and incorporating MAP features with guided LDA and weighted Gibbs sampling to produce enhanced multi-phenotype predictions for large-scale phenome-wide association studies.

Figure 2: Comprehensive flowchart illustrating simplified, LDA, and complex data generation methods for phenotyping algorithm evaluation. See Supplemental Tables S1-S3 for complete parameter specifications
Table 3: Performance metrics for phenotyping algorithms in a rare, informative scenario. This represents a real-world, complex case where these algorithms might be deployed.
Figure 3: Algorithm performance measured by AUC across different scenarios and data types. Error bars represent one standard deviation.
Figure 4: Algorithm performance measured by mean squared error (MSE) across different scenarios and data types. Error bars represent one standard deviation.

Table 1: Phenotyping algorithm versions. v2 variants use implementation details from sureLDA: PheNorm-v2 uses data-adaptive rather than fixed variance initialization; MAPv2 fits only on filter-positive patients rather than all patients.
Table 4: Comparison of Patient Characteristics Across Different Phenotyping Methods
